items.q: What does inheritance allow a subclass to do?
items.correct: Reuse and extend the attributes and methods of its superclass.
items.random_wrong: Copy code from any unrelated class.
items.why: Students think inheritance overrides everything. It actually REUSES the parent's members (which can be added to or overridden), creating an 'is-a' relationship.

items.q: What is abstraction in computer science?
items.correct: Removing unnecessary detail to focus on essential features of a problem.
items.misconception: Making something abstract art or vague.
items.random_wrong: Writing code that does not work.
items.why: Students borrow the everyday meaning of 'vague'. In CS, abstraction is a precise technique: ignoring details that don't matter for the current task, while keeping what does.

items.q: What is the purpose of database normalisation?
items.correct: To reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies.
items.misconception: To make the database run faster.
items.random_wrong: To make the database look neater.
items.why: Students think normalisation is about performance. In fact, heavy normalisation often slows queries. Its purpose is DATA INTEGRITY - preventing inconsistencies by storing each fact once.

items.q: Ziegler-Nichols PID tuning method was published in:
items.why: Correct answer: 1942. “1958” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A thermostat is an example of:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Negative feedback. “Positive feedback” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year Ziegler and Nichols published PID tuning method?
items.why: Exact answer: 1942 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
